addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Supabase
Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
/worksp/monitor
---ENV EXAMPLE---
# Synology Monitor Agent Configuration
# Copy this to .env and fill in your values

# NAS Identity (unique per NAS)
NAS_ID=nas-1
NAS_NAME=Synology NAS 1

# DSM API credentials
DSM_URL=https://localhost:5001
DSM_USERNAME=your_dsm_username
DSM_PASSWORD=your_dsm_password
DSM_INSECURE_SKIP_VERIFY=true

# Supabase connection
SUPABASE_URL=https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co
SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY=your_supabase_service_key

# Collection intervals (optional, defaults shown)
# METRICS_INTERVAL=30s
# STORAGE_INTERVAL=60s
# LOG_INTERVAL=10s
# DOCKER_INTERVAL=30s
# SECURITY_INTERVAL=15m

# File system monitoring (optional)
# LOG_DIR=/host/log
# WATCH_PATHS=/host/volume1,/host/volume2
# CHECKSUM_PATHS=/host/volume1,/host/volume2
# MAX_INOTIFY_DIRS=5000
# DATA_DIR=/app/data
# BATCH_SIZE=100
# FLUSH_TIMEOUT=30s
# MAX_WAL_SIZE_MB=100
---WEB ENV EXAMPLE---
# Supabase
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=your_anon_key_here
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=your_service_role_key_here

# OpenAI NAS copilot
OPENAI_API_KEY=your_openai_api_key
OPENAI_CHAT_MODEL=gpt-5.4

# Optional but recommended. If omitted, the server falls back to OPENAI_API_KEY
# to sign approval tokens for one-off NAS actions.
COPILOT_ACTION_SIGNING_KEY=replace_with_a_random_secret
COPILOT_ADMIN_EMAILS=admin@example.com,ops@example.com

# Issue worker
# inline = request path drains issue jobs
# background = dedicated worker endpoint/loop drains issue jobs
ISSUE_WORKER_MODE=inline
RUN_ISSUE_WORKER=false
ISSUE_WORKER_TOKEN=replace_with_a_random_secret
ISSUE_WORKER_INTERVAL_MS=3000
ISSUE_WORKER_BATCH_LIMIT=10

# NAS SSH access over Tailscale (used by legacy copilot chat only)
NAS_EDGE1_HOST=100.107.131.35
NAS_EDGE1_PORT=22
NAS_EDGE1_USER=popdam
NAS_EDGE1_PASSWORD=your_edge1_ssh_password
NAS_EDGE1_SUDO_PASSWORD=your_edge1_sudo_password

NAS_EDGE2_HOST=100.107.131.36
NAS_EDGE2_PORT=1904
NAS_EDGE2_USER=popdam
NAS_EDGE2_PASSWORD=your_edge2_ssh_password
NAS_EDGE2_SUDO_PASSWORD=your_edge2_sudo_password

# NAS API — three-tier HTTP shell execution (used by issue agent)
# URL = Tailscale IP of each NAS + the NAS_API_PORT set in the NAS .env
# SECRET and SIGNING_KEY must match NAS_API_SECRET and NAS_API_APPROVAL_SIGNING_KEY in each NAS .env
NAS_EDGE1_API_URL=http://100.107.131.35:7734
NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET=__SET_IN_COOLIFY__
NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY=__SET_IN_COOLIFY__

NAS_EDGE2_API_URL=http://100.107.131.36:7734
NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET=__SET_IN_COOLIFY__
NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY=__SET_IN_COOLIFY__

# Push Notifications (generate with: npx web-push generate-vapid-keys)
NEXT_PUBLIC_VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY=your_vapid_public_key

lastPrompt: move this application's supabase.com backend from the current project (in the Ohio DC) to new project https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co in virginia

aiTitle: Migrate Supabase backend to Virginia project

content.text: Tooling is good: `pg_dump`/`pg_restore`/`psql` (PG16) and `supabase` CLI 2.98.2 are all available, and there are no edge functions to move. The schema is fully captured in 41 migration files. The blocker for bulk data transfer is DB credentials. Let me check what credentials are actually available before I plan the data copy.

input.command:
cd /worksp/monitor
echo "=== actual .env files present (not examples) ==="
find app -name '.env' -o -name '.env.local' -o -name '.env.production' 2>/dev/null | grep -v Permission
echo
echo "=== supabase CLI link state ==="
ls -la app/supabase/ 2>/dev/null | grep -iE "config|\.temp|\.branches"
cat app/supabase/config.toml 2>/dev/null | head -20
echo
echo "=== env vars in shell that might hold creds ==="
env | grep -iE "SUPABASE|PG|DATABASE|POSTGRES" | sed 's/=.*/=<set>/'
echo
echo "=== relay recovery prompt (creds context) ==="
sed -n '60,160p' app/apps/relay/RECOVERY_PROMPT.md 2>/dev/null
input.description: Check for available DB credentials and CLI link state

content.content:
=== actual .env files present (not examples) ===

=== supabase CLI link state ===

=== env vars in shell that might hold creds ===
LS_COLORS=<set>
SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN=<set>

=== relay recovery prompt (creds context) ===

- `NAS_EDGE1_API_SECRET`
  - `__REDACTED__`
- `NAS_EDGE1_API_SIGNING_KEY`
  - `__REDACTED__`
- `NAS_EDGE2_API_SECRET`
  - `__REDACTED__`
- `NAS_EDGE2_API_SIGNING_KEY`
  - `__REDACTED__`

## Supabase

- project url:
  - `https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co`
- service role key:
  - `__REDACTED__`

## Relay secrets

- `RELAY_BEARER_TOKEN`
  - `__REDACTED__`
- `RELAY_ADMIN_SECRET`
  - `__REDACTED__`

## Current live state

- NAS API live mounts were expanded on both NASes
- live NAS API now has access to:
  - `/proc`
  - `/sys`
  - `/etc/passwd`
  - `/var/log`
  - `/var/packages`
  - main `/volume1/...` shares
  - `/volume1/@synologydrive`
  - `/volume1/@SynologyDriveShareSync`
  - `/volume1/@appdata/HyperBackup`
  - `/volume1/docker/synology-monitor-agent` as read-only
  - `/var/run/docker.sock`
- Hyper Backup backfill already succeeded once:
  - `edgesynology1`: inserted `2104`
  - `edgesynology2`: inserted `2114`

## Important caveat

The repo contains newer NAS API hardening code, but that code is not live until the NAS API image is rebuilt and redeployed.

Files to review:

- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-api/Dockerfile`
- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-api/cmd/server/main.go`
- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/nas-api/internal/validator/validator.go`

## Relay files to review

- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/relay/src/server.mjs`
- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/relay/README.md`
- `/worksp/monitor/app/apps/relay/OPERATIONS.md`

## What to do first in a recovery session

1. Verify relay health if deployed.
2. Verify NAS API health on both NASes.
3. Confirm NAS API mounts on both NASes.
4. Confirm Hyper Backup is not currently in a sensitive state before any container restarts.
5. If NAS API code changes need to go live, rebuild and redeploy only `synology-monitor-nas-api`.

## Verification commands

### NAS API health

```bash
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer __REDACTED__' \
  http://100.107.131.35:7734/health

curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer __REDACTED__' \
  http://100.107.131.36:7734/health
```

### Inspect live mounts on a NAS

```bash
sshpass -p '__REDACTED__' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no popdam@100.107.131.35 \
  "printf '%s\n' '__REDACTED__' | sudo -S /var/packages/ContainerManager/target/usr/bin/docker inspect synology-monitor-nas-api --format '{{json .Mounts}}'"
```

### Backfill command

```bash
cd /worksp/monitor/app
SUPABASE_URL='https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co' \
SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY='__REDACTED__' \
node scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs
```

## Relay env to use

```env
PORT=8787
RELAY_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://your-lovable-app.example.com
RELAY_BEARER_TOKEN=__REDACTED__

input.prompt:
Explore the SQL migration files in /worksp/monitor/app/supabase/migrations/ (files 00001 through 00041). I'm migrating this Supabase Postgres database to a brand-new Supabase project, so I need to find everything that is tied to the CURRENT project and would have to be rewritten or recreated for the new one.

For each finding, report the exact file path, line number, and the relevant SQL snippet. Specifically hunt for:

1. **pg_cron jobs** — any `cron.schedule(...)` / `cron.schedule_in_database(...)` calls. List the job name, schedule, and the full command each job runs.
2. **pg_net / http calls** — any `net.http_post`, `net.http_get`, `http_post`, `http(...)`, or similar. Capture the URL and headers, especially if a Supabase project URL (e.g. https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co) or a service-role key / Authorization bearer token is embedded.
3. **Vault usage** — any `vault.create_secret`, `vault.update_secret`, `vault.decrypted_secrets`, references to `supabase_vault`, or secret names. These secrets do NOT transfer with a data dump and must be recreated.
4. **Hardcoded project identifiers** — any literal occurrence of the project ref `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn`, a full supabase.co URL, a JWT/service key, or a region.
5. **SECURITY DEFINER functions** and the `exec_sql` function (migration 00010) — list them by name; note any that reference current_setting of app settings, GUCs, or project-specific config.
6. **pg_partman configuration** — how partitioned tables are set up (part_config inserts, `create_parent` calls, retention/premake settings, the maintenance cron job).
7. **Extensions** — every `CREATE EXTENSION` statement and the schema it targets.

Be thorough and exhaustive — read every migration file. Return an organized summary grouped by the 7 categories above. Do not modify anything.

input.prompt:
I'm repointing this application from one Supabase project to another. I need a COMPLETE inventory of every place the codebase connects to or references Supabase, so nothing is missed during cutover. Explore /worksp/monitor/app thoroughly.

For each component, report file paths and the exact env var names / config keys used:

1. **Web app (apps/web)** — how it initializes the Supabase client (browser + server). Find files under apps/web/src/lib/supabase/ and anywhere SUPABASE/NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE env vars are read. List every env var name (URL, anon/publishable key, service-role key). Note the Dockerfile and any build-time vs runtime env distinction.
2. **NAS monitor agent** — the Go/other agent that writes telemetry. How does it get SUPABASE_URL / SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY? Look in deploy/synology/*.env.example and the agent source. List the env var names.
3. **Relay (apps/relay)** — does it talk to Supabase? Which env vars?
4. **Scripts** — anything in app/scripts/ that connects to Supabase (e.g. backfill scripts), and the env vars / default URLs they use.
5. **Deployment/config** — any Coolify config, docker-compose, Dockerfiles, GitHub Actions workflows, or .env.example files that hardcode the project URL https://qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn.supabase.co or set Supabase env vars. List each file.
6. **Docs** — list doc files that mention the project ref qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn (so they can be updated).

Return an organized inventory grouped by component, with every env var name and every file that contains the old project URL/ref. This is for building a cutover runbook — be exhaustive. Do not modify anything.

content.text:
Perfect. Now let me create a comprehensive summary of all the findings organized by the seven categories:

## Comprehensive Migration Audit: Project-Specific Dependencies

I have completed an exhaustive read of all 41 Supabase migrations (00001 through 00041). Below is the organized summary of everything tied to the **current** project that must be rewritten or recreated for a new Supabase instance:

---

### 1. **PG_CRON JOBS**

All pg_cron jobs are scheduled in migration files. Current jobs (as of migration 00034):

| File | Line | Job Name | Schedule | Command | Notes |
|------|------|----------|----------|---------|-------|
| 00003 | 66–70 | `smon-partition-maintenance` | `0 7 * * *` (daily 7 AM UTC) | `SELECT public.run_maintenance_proc()` | Runs pg_partman maintenance |
| 00005 | 298–302 | `smon-anomaly-detection` | `*/15 * * * *` (every 15 min) | `SELECT smon_run_anomaly_detection()` | Calls OpenAI API for metric anomalies |
| 00005 | 305–309 | `smon-daily-health` | `0 12 * * *` (daily 12 PM UTC) | `SELECT smon_run_daily_health()` | Calls OpenAI API for health report |
| 00005 | 312–316 | `smon-process-ai-responses` | `* * * * *` (every minute) | `SELECT smon_process_ai_responses()` | Polls pg_net HTTP responses |
| 00009 | 288–292 | `smon-sync-anomaly-detection` | `*/15 * * * *` (every 15 min) | `SELECT smon_detect_sync_anomalies()` | Detects sync conflicts/errors |
| 00009 | 295–299 | `smon-sync-health-analysis` | `0 */4 * * *` (every 4 hours) | `SELECT smon_analyze_sync_health()` | Analyzes sync health patterns |
| 00009 | 302–306 | `smon-weekly-sync-report` | `0 8 * * 0` (Sunday 8 AM UTC) | `SELECT smon_generate_weekly_sync_report()` | Weekly sync report |
| 00011 | 323–327 | `smon-sync-remediation` | `*/15 * * * *` (every 15 min) | `SELECT smon_run_sync_remediation()` | Detects & flags sync issues for review |

**Migration 00034 renames the jobs** (lines 554–561) but keeps the same schedules:
- `partition-maintenance`, `anomaly-detection`, `daily-health`, `process-ai-responses`, `sync-anomaly-detection`, `sync-health-analysis`, `weekly-sync-report`, `sync-remediation`

---

### 2. **PG_NET / HTTP CALLS**

All pg_net HTTP calls are made from SECURITY DEFINER functions that call the **OpenAI API**:

| File | Lines | Function | HTTP Method | URL | Headers | Notes |
|------|-------|----------|------------|-----|---------|-------|
| 00005 | 119–134 | `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` | `net.http_post()` | `https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions` | `Content-Type: application/json`, `Authorization: Bearer <api_key_from_vault>` | Posts anomaly-detection prompt; uses vault secret `smon_openai_api_key` |
| 00005 | 196–211 | `smon_run_daily_health()` | `net.http_post()` | `https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions` | `Content-Type: application/json`, `Authorization: Bearer <api_key_from_vault>` | Posts daily health report prompt |

**Both functions use:**
- **Model:** `'gpt-5.4-mini'` (hardcoded in 00005, migration 00015/00017 adds configurable settings via `ai_settings` table)
- **Vault secret name:** `smon_openai_api_key` (referenced in 00005 line 19, updated in 00031 line 122)
- **Response polling:** `smon_process_ai_responses()` polls `net._http_response` (00005 line 232, updated in 00031 line 263)

---

### 3. **VAULT USAGE**

| File | Lines | Operation | Secret Name | Details |
|------|-------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| 00005 | 5–6 (comment) | Manual insert | `openai_api_key` | Comment shows: `insert into vault.secrets (name, secret) values ('openai_api_key', 'sk-...')` — **must be recreated manually** |
| 00005 | 9–23 | `smon_get_openai_key()` function | `smon_openai_api_key` | Reads from `vault.decrypted_secrets WHERE name = 'smon_openai_api_key'` |
| 00031 | 117–125 | Updated function after rename | `smon_openai_api_key` | Same vault read in migration 00031 (post-rename) |

**Critical:** Vault secrets **do not transfer** with a data dump. The secret must be recreated in the new project:
```sql
INSERT INTO vault.secrets (name, secret) VALUES ('smon_openai_api_key', '<new-key>');
```

---

### 4. **HARDCODED PROJECT IDENTIFIERS**

**Search results:**
- No literal occurrences of project ref `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` or Supabase URLs found in migration files.
- No hardcoded JWT keys, service keys, or region identifiers in migrations.

**However, there ARE project-specific identifiers in the database:**

| File | Lines | Identifier | Type | Context |
|------|-------|-----------|------|---------|
| 00040 | 44, 57 | `edgesynology1` | NAS unit name | Hardcoded in `custom_metric_schedules` INSERT for disk health job |
| 00040 | 61, 68 | `edgesynology2` | NAS unit name | Hardcoded in `custom_metric_schedules` INSERT for disk health job |

These are **NAS names** (the agent's logical units), not Supabase project refs. They will need to be updated if the operator runs migration 00040 with different NAS names.

---

### 5. **SECURITY DEFINER FUNCTIONS & `exec_sql`**

All SECURITY DEFINER functions (run with schema owner privileges) that reference current_setting or GUC:

| File | Lines | Function Name | Purpose | References |
|------|-------|---------------|---------|------------|
| 00005 | 9–23 | `smon_get_openai_key()` | SECURITY DEFINER; retrieves vault secret | Reads `vault.decrypted_secrets` |
| 00005 | 26–44 | `smon_create_alert(...)` | Creates alerts from findings | No current_setting refs |
| 00005 | 47–137 | `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` | SECURITY DEFINER; calls OpenAI API via pg_net | Calls `smon_get_openai_key()` |
| 00005 | 140–213 | `smon_run_daily_health()` | SECURITY DEFINER; calls OpenAI API | Calls `smon_get_openai_key()` |
| 00005 | 216–294 | `smon_process_ai_responses()` | SECURITY DEFINER; polls pg_net responses | Reads `net._http_response` |
| 00009 | 8–134 | `smon_detect_sync_anomalies()` | SECURITY DEFINER; detects sync issues | No current_setting refs |
| 00009 | 137–213 | `smon_analyze_sync_health()` | SECURITY DEFINER; queues sync analysis | Calls `smon_get_openai_key()` |
| 00009 | 216–285 | `smon_generate_weekly_sync_report()` | SECURITY DEFINER; builds weekly report | No current_setting refs |
| 00011 | 35–74 | `smon_detect_stuck_syncs()` | SECURITY DEFINER; returns table of stuck syncs | No current_setting refs |
| 00011 | 77–118 | `smon_detect_sync_conflicts()` | SECURITY DEFINER; returns table of conflicts | No current_setting refs |
| 00011 | 121–157 | `smon_detect_invalid_chars()` | SECURITY DEFINER; returns table of invalid chars | No current_setting refs |
| 00011 | 160–195 | `smon_detect_lock_timeouts()` | SECURITY DEFINER; returns table of lock issues | No current_setting refs |
| 00011 | 198–320 | `smon_run_sync_remediation()` | SECURITY DEFINER; main remediation orchestrator | No current_setting refs |
| 00010 | 5–14 | **`exec_sql(sql text)`** | **CRITICAL:** Arbitrary SQL execution | **SECURITY DEFINER; grants EXECUTE to service_role** |

**`exec_sql` special note** (Migration 00010, lines 5–17):
```sql
CREATE FUNCTION exec_sql(sql text) RETURNS void 
  LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER SET search_path = public AS $$
BEGIN EXECUTE sql; END; $$;
GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION exec_sql(text) TO service_role;
```
This function allows the service role to execute **arbitrary SQL**. It is intentionally designed for API-driven schema changes. **No GUC/current_setting references.**

**All other SECURITY DEFINER functions updated in migration 00031** to reference renamed tables (smon_* → unprefixed names).

---

### 6. **PG_PARTMAN CONFIGURATION**

| File | Lines | Configuration | Details |
|------|-------|---------------|---------|
| 00001 | 4 | Extension creation | `CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_partman SCHEMA public;` |
| 00003 | 6–12 | `smon_metrics` parent creation | `create_parent(..., p_interval := '7 days', p_premake := 2, retention := '84 days')` |
| 00003 | 14–18 | Config update | `UPDATE part_config WHERE parent_table = 'public.smon_metrics' SET retention = '84 days'` |
| 00003 | 21–27 | `smon_logs` parent creation | `create_parent(..., p_interval := '7 days', p_premake := 2, retention := '180 days')` |
| 00003 | 29–33 | Config update | `UPDATE part_config WHERE parent_table = 'public.smon_logs' SET retention = '180 days'` |
| 00003 | 36–42 | `smon_storage_snapshots` parent | `create_parent(..., p_interval := '7 days', p_premake := 2, retention := '84 days')` |
| 00003 | 44–48 | Config update | `UPDATE part_config WHERE parent_table = 'public.smon_storage_snapshots' SET retention = '84 days'` |
| 00003 | 51–57 | `smon_container_status` parent | `create_parent(..., p_interval := '7 days', p_premake := 2, retention := '180 days')` |
| 00003 | 59–63 | Config update | `UPDATE part_config WHERE parent_table = 'public.smon_container_status' SET retention = '180 days'` |
| 00003 | 66–70 | Maintenance cron | Scheduled `cron.schedule('smon-partition-maintenance', '0 7 * * *', ...)` |

**Key partman settings:**
- **Partitioning interval:** 7 days (weekly partitions)
- **Premake:** 2 (pre-creates 2 future partitions)
- **Retention:** varies (84 days for metrics/storage, 180 days for logs/containers)
- **Maintenance job:** Runs daily at 7 AM UTC via `public.run_maintenance_proc()`

**After migration 00031 table renames:**
- `smon_metrics` → `metrics`
- `smon_logs` → `nas_logs`
- `smon_storage_snapshots` → `storage_snapshots`
- `smon_container_status` → `container_status`

**Partman config table:** `part_config` (system table managed by pg_partman; contains retention, maintenance job ID)

---

### 7. **EXTENSIONS**

| File | Lines | Extension | Schema | Notes |
|------|-------|-----------|--------|-------|
| 00001 | 4 | `pg_partman` | `public` | Enables partition management for time-series tables |

**Additional extensions used (assumed pre-installed by Supabase):**
- `pg_cron` — scheduling (pre-installed; jobs defined in migrations 00003, 00005, 00009, 00011)
- `pg_net` — HTTP client (pre-installed; used in 00005 for OpenAI calls)
- `vault` — secret management (pre-installed; referenced in 00005, 00031)

---

### SUMMARY OF MIGRATION EFFORT FOR NEW PROJECT

**Must recreate (manual steps outside migrations):**
1. **Vault secret:** `smon_openai_api_key` with the new OpenAI API key
2. **NAS names** if using different identifiers than `edgesynology1` / `edgesynology2` (update migration 00040 or re-run with new names)

**Handled by migrations (idempotent re-apply):**
1. All extensions, tables, indexes, RLS policies
2. All functions (both standard and SECURITY DEFINER)
3. All pg_cron jobs (00034 re-registers them after table renames)
4. All pg_partman configurations
5. All pg_net HTTP call infrastructure (function bodies remain unchanged)

**Verify/test post-migration:**
1. OpenAI API key is correctly stored in vault and accessible by `smon_get_openai_key()`
2. pg_cron jobs are active (check `cron.job` table)
3. pg_partman maintenance job runs and creates weekly partitions
4. pg_net HTTP calls succeed (test `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` manually)
5. NAS names in custom_metric_schedules match your actual NAS units

---

**All files read (complete audit):**  
`/worksp/monitor/app/supabase/migrations/00001` through `00041`  
Total lines analyzed: ~3,500+

**Critical:** Vault secrets **do not transfer** with a data dump. The secret must be recreated in the new project:
```sql
INSERT INTO vault.secrets (name, secret) VALUES ('smon_openai_api_key', '<new-key>');
```

---

### 4. **HARDCODED PROJECT IDENTIFIERS**

**Search results:**
- No literal occurrences of project ref `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` or Supabase URLs found in migration files.

| 00040 | 61, 68 | `edgesynology2` | NAS unit name | Hardcoded in `custom_metric_schedules` INSERT for disk health job |

These are **NAS names** (the agent's logical units), not Supabase project refs. They will need to be updated if the operator runs migration 00040 with different NAS names.

---

### 5. **SECURITY DEFINER FUNCTIONS & `exec_sql`**

All SECURITY DEFINER functions (run with schema owner privileges) that reference current_setting or GUC:

| File | Lines | Function Name | Purpose | References |
|------|-------|---------------|---------|------------|
| 00005 | 9–23 | `smon_get_openai_key()` | SECURITY DEFINER; retrieves vault secret | Reads `vault.decrypted_secrets` |
| 00005 | 26–44 | `smon_create_alert(...)` | Creates alerts from findings | No current_setting refs |
| 00005 | 47–137 | `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` | SECURITY DEFINER; calls OpenAI API via pg_net | Calls `smon_get_openai_key()` |
| 00005 | 140–213 | `smon_run_daily_health()` | SECURITY DEFINER; calls OpenAI API | Calls `smon_get_openai_key()` |

| 00009 | 216–285 | `smon_generate_weekly_sync_report()` | SECURITY DEFINER; builds weekly report | No current_setting refs |

| 00010 | 5–14 | **`exec_sql(sql text)`** | **CRITICAL:** Arbitrary SQL execution | **SECURITY DEFINER; grants EXECUTE to service_role** |

**`exec_sql` special note** (Migration 00010, lines 5–17):
```sql
CREATE FUNCTION exec_sql(sql text) RETURNS void 
  LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER SET search_path = public AS $$
BEGIN EXECUTE sql; END; $$;
GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION exec_sql(text) TO service_role;
```
This function allows the service role to execute **arbitrary SQL**. It is intentionally designed for API-driven schema changes. **No GUC/current_setting references.**

**All other SECURITY DEFINER functions updated in migration 00031** to reference renamed tables (smon_* → unprefixed names).

| 00003 | 51–57 | `smon_container_status` parent | `create_parent(..., p_interval := '7 days', p_premake := 2, retention := '180 days')` |
| 00003 | 59–63 | Config update | `UPDATE part_config WHERE parent_table = 'public.smon_container_status' SET retention = '180 days'` |
| 00003 | 66–70 | Maintenance cron | Scheduled `cron.schedule('smon-partition-maintenance', '0 7 * * *', ...)` |

**Key partman settings:**
- **Partitioning interval:** 7 days (weekly partitions)
- **Premake:** 2 (pre-creates 2 future partitions)
- **Retention:** varies (84 days for metrics/storage, 180 days for logs/containers)
- **Maintenance job:** Runs daily at 7 AM UTC via `public.run_maintenance_proc()`

**After migration 00031 table renames:**
- `smon_metrics` → `metrics`
- `smon_logs` → `nas_logs`
- `smon_storage_snapshots` → `storage_snapshots`
- `smon_container_status` → `container_status`

**Partman config table:** `part_config` (system table managed by pg_partman; contains retention, maintenance job ID)

### 7. **EXTENSIONS**

| File | Lines | Extension | Schema | Notes |
|------|-------|-----------|--------|-------|
| 00001 | 4 | `pg_partman` | `public` | Enables partition management for time-series tables |

- `pg_cron` — scheduling (pre-installed; jobs defined in migrations 00003, 00005, 00009, 00011)
- `pg_net` — HTTP client (pre-installed; used in 00005 for OpenAI calls)
- `vault` — secret management (pre-installed; referenced in 00005, 00031)

**Must recreate (manual steps outside migrations):**
1. **Vault secret:** `smon_openai_api_key` with the new OpenAI API key
2. **NAS names** if using different identifiers than `edgesynology1` / `edgesynology2` (update migration 00040 or re-run with new names)

**Handled by migrations (idempotent re-apply):**
1. All extensions, tables, indexes, RLS policies
2. All functions (both standard and SECURITY DEFINER)
3. All pg_cron jobs (00034 re-registers them after table renames)
4. All pg_partman configurations
5. All pg_net HTTP call infrastructure (function bodies remain unchanged)

**Verify/test post-migration:**
1. OpenAI API key is correctly stored in vault and accessible by `smon_get_openai_key()`
2. pg_cron jobs are active (check `cron.job` table)
3. pg_partman maintenance job runs and creates weekly partitions
4. pg_net HTTP calls succeed (test `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` manually)
5. NAS names in custom_metric_schedules match your actual NAS units

`/worksp/monitor/app/supabase/migrations/00001` through `00041`  
Total lines analyzed: ~3,500+

### **11. DEPLOYMENT CUTOVER CHECKLIST**

**Required changes (can break app if missed):**
1. Update GitHub Secrets: `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` → trigger new `web-image` build
2. Update Coolify env: `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` (web app server-side)
3. Update each NAS `.env` file: `SUPABASE_URL` and `SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`
4. Deploy new edge function with updated `SUPABASE_URL` and `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`

**Nice-to-have updates (documentation, dev ergonomics):**

2. Update `apply-migrations.ps1` (hardcoded URL)
3. Update docs: `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/development.md`
4. Update `scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs` (hardcoded fallback)
5. Update recovery docs: `apps/relay/RECOVERY_PROMPT.md`

**Testing after cutover:**

- Trigger agent telemetry collection on at least one NAS and verify rows appear in new Supabase
- Verify web app loads and displays data

- Backfill test: `SUPABASE_URL=... SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY=... node scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs`

This inventory is complete and exhaustive. No Supabase connections have been missed.

Excellent, thorough audits. Two important findings beyond my earlier scan:

- There **is** an edge function in the repo (`app/supabase/functions/send-push/index.ts`) but it's **not deployed** on the platform (MCP `list_edge_functions` returned empty) — so push is either inactive or runs elsewhere.
- The **vault secret `smon_openai_api_key`** and the **8 pg_cron jobs** live outside the dumped `public` schema and won't travel with a normal dump — they must be recreated explicitly. The in-DB AI cron jobs call OpenAI using that vault secret.
- The web app's `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`/`ANON_KEY` are **baked into the image at Docker build** via GitHub Actions secrets — a Coolify runtime env change won't repoint them; the image must be rebuilt.

Let me ground the dump/restore procedure in Supabase's official cross-project migration guide before finalizing.

content.content:
# Analysis of Provided Content

After reviewing the webpage content, I must report that **there are no verbatim pg_dump, supabase db dump, or psql commands present** in the material.

The document does reference these tools conceptually:

- **pg_dump**: Mentioned as "For smaller databases, we recommend taking a logical backup of the data using pg_dump utility" but no actual command syntax is provided.

- **pg_upgrade**: Referenced as the underlying mechanism used during upgrades ("Your data is copied to the new instance and upgraded using pg_upgrade") but presented as a background process, not a user-executable command.

- **pg_basebackup**: Mentioned as part of the upgrade validation process but not as a command users should run.

## What's Missing

The document does **not include**:
- Step-by-step migration procedures between projects
- Role migration SQL (beyond the md5 password caveat example)
- Schema or data transfer commands
- Auth user migration guidance
- Storage migration instructions
- Extension migration procedures

The only executable SQL provided addresses md5 password migration for custom roles post-upgrade.

**Conclusion**: This document focuses on Supabase's built-in upgrade process rather than manual database migration procedures, so it cannot fulfill your request for migration command sequences.

content.text: The doc pages only contain navigation, but the canonical Supabase CLI migration flow is well-established (`supabase db dump` for roles/schema/data + a single `psql` restore with `session_replication_role = replica`). I have everything I need. Let me write the plan.

input.content:
# Migrate Supabase backend: Ohio → Virginia

## Context

The Synology-monitor app's entire backend is a Supabase Postgres project in
**us-east-2 (Ohio)**: `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` ("SynoMon.old"). The owner wants it
moved to a freshly created, currently-empty project in **us-east-1 (Virginia)**:
`aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` ("synomon", https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co).

Decisions confirmed by the owner:
- **Full-history copy** — everything, including the ~70M+ rows of telemetry.
- I perform the migration end-to-end. Owner provides: both DB passwords, the
  Coolify API token, and confirms the Supabase CLI is already authenticated
  (`SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN` is in the environment).
- The app can be **paused (~1h from now)** to get a clean, low-drift snapshot.

What "the backend" comprises (verified): ~100 tables (4 pg_partman-managed
time-series sets with weekly partitions p20260404…p20260606, plus low-volume
config/state tables), 8 pg_cron jobs, SECURITY-DEFINER functions incl.
`exec_sql`, a `supabase_vault` secret, RLS on every table, and a small number of
auth users. **No storage buckets in use. No edge functions deployed** (one
exists in code, `supabase/functions/send-push`, but was never deployed).

Outcome: an identical, fully-populated project in Virginia, with the live web
app and both NAS agents writing to it, the repo/docs updated, and the old
project left intact as a rollback.

## Why dump/restore (not migration replay)

The 41 migration files build the schema, but replaying them then `COPY`-ing data
risks partition-routing mismatches (pg_partman premake vs. the exact historical
weekly partitions that exist in source). A structural `pg_dump` captures the
exact final state — every child partition, function, index, RLS policy, and the
`part_config` rows — so it is the faithful path. Three things live *outside* the
dumped `public` schema and must be recreated explicitly: **pg_cron jobs** (`cron`
schema), the **vault secret** (encrypted per-project), and **auth users**.

## Prerequisites to collect at execution start

- `SOURCE_DB_URL` and `TARGET_DB_URL` — full Postgres connection strings
  (owner-provided passwords). Prefer the **session pooler** host on port 5432 for
  `pg_dump`/`pg_restore`. Format:
  `postgresql://postgres.<ref>:<pw>@aws-0-<region>.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres`
- Coolify API base URL + token + the web app's application UUID.
- Confirm `gh` is authenticated to the GitHub repo that runs
  `.github/workflows/web-image.yml`, with permission to set Actions secrets.
- Confirm reachability to repoint both NAS agents (NAS API over Tailscale, or
  SSH creds) — they hold their own `SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`.
- Working scratch dir with tens of GB free (verify `df -h`).

## Phase 0 — Freeze (coordinated with owner)

1. Owner pauses the **web app** (stops web-originated writes).
2. Capture source baseline row counts for the verification diff:
   `SELECT relname, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY 1;` plus exact
   counts for the big tables. Record DB size (`pg_database_size`).
3. NAS agents buffer to a local WAL/SQLite queue, so they need not be stopped —
   any rows they write to OLD after the snapshot are a small, regenerating
   telemetry gap; their unflushed buffer flushes to NEW after repoint (Phase 5).
   Note the snapshot time so the gap is understood.

## Phase 1 — Prepare the target project

Enable the extensions the schema depends on, in the same schemas as source
(verified installed on source: `pg_partman`@public, `pg_cron`@pg_catalog,
`pg_net`@public, `supabase_vault`@vault, `pgcrypto`/`uuid-ossp`/
`pg_stat_statements`@extensions, `plpgsql`). Most are created by the schema dump
or pre-exist on Supabase; create any missing ones via `apply_migration` (runs as
the privileged role). Confirm with `list_extensions(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)`.

## Phase 2 — Dump the source

Run from the scratch dir against `SOURCE_DB_URL`:

```bash
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f roles.sql  --role-only
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f schema.sql
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f data.sql   --use-copy --data-only
```

Then capture the out-of-band objects the above three files do **not** include:

- **Auth users** (needed for login + RLS + FK references):
  `pg_dump "$SOURCE_DB_URL" --data-only --schema=auth -t auth.users -t auth.identities -t auth.mfa_factors -f auth_data.sql`
  (enumerate actual non-empty `auth.*` tables first; keep it to user data, not
  Supabase-managed migration/bookkeeping tables).
- **pg_cron jobs** — dump the live job set so schedules/commands match exactly:
  `SELECT jobname, schedule, command FROM cron.job ORDER BY jobid;`
  Expect 8 jobs (renamed by migration 00034): `partition-maintenance`,
  `anomaly-detection`, `daily-health`, `process-ai-responses`,
  `sync-anomaly-detection`, `sync-health-analysis`, `weekly-sync-report`,
  `sync-remediation`. Generate `cron.schedule(...)` statements from the result.
- **Vault secret** — read the decrypted value from source to re-insert in target:
  `SELECT name, decrypted_secret FROM vault.decrypted_secrets WHERE name='smon_openai_api_key';`
  (If decryption is blocked, ask the owner for the OpenAI key value instead.)

For the very large data dump, prefer speed/robustness: dump in directory format
with parallelism for the big tables, or pipe `pg_dump | psql` per large table, to
avoid a single tens-of-GB SQL file. Validate disk before choosing; `--use-copy`
plain SQL is the safe default if disk allows.

## Phase 3 — Restore into the target

Canonical Supabase restore order, single transaction, triggers/FKs deferred:

```bash
psql --single-transaction --variable ON_ERROR_STOP=1 \
  --file roles.sql \
  --file schema.sql \
  --command 'SET session_replication_role = replica' \
  --file data.sql \
  --dbname "$TARGET_DB_URL"
```

Then load `auth_data.sql` (also with `session_replication_role = replica`).
`roles.sql` should be near-empty (no custom roles); ignore "already exists" on
Supabase default roles if any. After load, reset sequences to max(id) for any
serial PKs that were data-loaded.

## Phase 4 — Recreate out-of-band objects on target

1. **Vault secret**: `SELECT vault.create_secret('<value>', 'smon_openai_api_key');`
   Verify `smon_get_openai_key()` returns it.
2. **pg_cron jobs**: replay the 8 `cron.schedule(...)` statements captured in
   Phase 2. Verify `SELECT jobname, schedule FROM cron.job;` shows all 8.
3. **pg_partman**: confirm `part_config` rows restored (4 parents) and run one
   maintenance pass — `CALL public.run_maintenance_proc();` — then confirm it
   creates the next premake partitions without error.
4. Sanity-check `exec_sql` and the SECURITY-DEFINER `smon_*` functions exist and
   `service_role` has EXECUTE where expected.
5. Run `get_advisors(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)` for security/performance lints.

## Phase 5 — Repoint the application

New project values to wire in (fetch at execution):
- URL: `https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co`
- anon/publishable key: via `get_publishable_keys` / `supabase projects api-keys`
- service_role (secret) key: via `supabase projects api-keys --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`

**a. Repo + docs** (single ref swap `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` → `aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`):
`app/.env.example`, `apps/web/.env.example`, `deploy/synology/{nas-1,nas-2,.env.agent}.example`,
`scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs` (DEFAULT_SUPABASE_URL), `apply-migrations.ps1`,
`AGENTS.md`, `docs/{architecture,deployment,development}.md`,
`apps/relay/RECOVERY_PROMPT.md`. Commit on a branch.

**b. Web app image (baked at build!)**: `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and
`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are compiled into the bundle via
`.github/workflows/web-image.yml` build args from GitHub Secrets — a Coolify
runtime change does **not** repoint them. So: `gh secret set` both values →
trigger the workflow (`gh workflow run web-image.yml`) → wait for the new image.

**c. Coolify**: set runtime env on the web service — `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`
(and `SUPABASE_URL`/`NEXT_PUBLIC_*` if also set at runtime) to the new project —
then redeploy so it pulls the freshly built image.

**d. NAS agents (both)**: update each agent's `SUPABASE_URL` +
`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY` and restart the container. On restart they flush their
buffered queue into the new project. Repoint NAS-1 and NAS-2.

**e. Edge function (optional)**: only if push is wanted — `supabase functions
deploy send-push --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` and set its
`SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` secrets. Skipping changes nothing
today (it isn't deployed on the old project either).

## Phase 6 — Verify end-to-end

- **Data parity**: compare per-table counts target vs. the Phase 0 baseline
  (allow only the known post-snapshot telemetry delta). Spot-check a few config
  tables (`ai_settings`, `issues`, `copilot_*`, `facts`, `capability_state`) for
  exact equality.
- **App**: web app loads, login works (auth users migrated), dashboards render.
- **Agents**: confirm fresh rows landing in the new project's `metrics` /
  `nas_logs` for both NAS units (proves write path repointed).
- **In-DB automation**: confirm cron jobs are scheduled and a maintenance run +
  an `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` test call succeed (proves pg_cron + pg_net +
  vault wired). Run `scripts/check-dashboard-data.mjs` against the new URL/key.

## Phase 7 — Decommission / rollback

- Keep the old Ohio project **paused but intact** for a retention window as the
  rollback. Rollback = revert env on Coolify + NAS agents (and GH secrets +
  rebuild) to the old project; data written to NEW during the window would be the
  only loss.
- Once stable, owner can rename/pause/delete the old project. Separately, the
  pre-existing leaked-secrets rotation item still stands and is unaffected here.

## Risks / watch-items

- **Baked anon key**: forgetting the rebuild leaves the web bundle pointing at
  Ohio even after Coolify env changes — most likely footgun.
- **Cross-region volume**: tens of GB Ohio→sandbox→Virginia; size the dump
  strategy to disk/bandwidth, use `--use-copy`/parallel, expect a multi-hour
  data phase.
- **Vault + cron are silent if missed**: AI analyses/health just stop with no
  app error. Phase 4 + Phase 6 explicitly test them.
- **Auth**: if users aren't migrated, nobody can log in to the web app.
- Keep `SET session_replication_role = replica` for both data loads or FK/trigger
  ordering will fail the restore.

toolUseResult.content:
# Migrate Supabase backend: Ohio → Virginia

## Context

The Synology-monitor app's entire backend is a Supabase Postgres project in
**us-east-2 (Ohio)**: `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` ("SynoMon.old"). The owner wants it
moved to a freshly created, currently-empty project in **us-east-1 (Virginia)**:
`aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` ("synomon", https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co).

Decisions confirmed by the owner:
- **Full-history copy** — everything, including the ~70M+ rows of telemetry.
- I perform the migration end-to-end. Owner provides: both DB passwords, the
  Coolify API token, and confirms the Supabase CLI is already authenticated
  (`SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN` is in the environment).
- The app can be **paused (~1h from now)** to get a clean, low-drift snapshot.

What "the backend" comprises (verified): ~100 tables (4 pg_partman-managed
time-series sets with weekly partitions p20260404…p20260606, plus low-volume
config/state tables), 8 pg_cron jobs, SECURITY-DEFINER functions incl.
`exec_sql`, a `supabase_vault` secret, RLS on every table, and a small number of
auth users. **No storage buckets in use. No edge functions deployed** (one
exists in code, `supabase/functions/send-push`, but was never deployed).

Outcome: an identical, fully-populated project in Virginia, with the live web
app and both NAS agents writing to it, the repo/docs updated, and the old
project left intact as a rollback.

## Why dump/restore (not migration replay)

The 41 migration files build the schema, but replaying them then `COPY`-ing data
risks partition-routing mismatches (pg_partman premake vs. the exact historical
weekly partitions that exist in source). A structural `pg_dump` captures the
exact final state — every child partition, function, index, RLS policy, and the
`part_config` rows — so it is the faithful path. Three things live *outside* the
dumped `public` schema and must be recreated explicitly: **pg_cron jobs** (`cron`
schema), the **vault secret** (encrypted per-project), and **auth users**.

## Prerequisites to collect at execution start

- `SOURCE_DB_URL` and `TARGET_DB_URL` — full Postgres connection strings
  (owner-provided passwords). Prefer the **session pooler** host on port 5432 for
  `pg_dump`/`pg_restore`. Format:
  `postgresql://postgres.<ref>:<pw>@aws-0-<region>.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres`
- Coolify API base URL + token + the web app's application UUID.
- Confirm `gh` is authenticated to the GitHub repo that runs
  `.github/workflows/web-image.yml`, with permission to set Actions secrets.
- Confirm reachability to repoint both NAS agents (NAS API over Tailscale, or
  SSH creds) — they hold their own `SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`.
- Working scratch dir with tens of GB free (verify `df -h`).

## Phase 0 — Freeze (coordinated with owner)

1. Owner pauses the **web app** (stops web-originated writes).
2. Capture source baseline row counts for the verification diff:
   `SELECT relname, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY 1;` plus exact
   counts for the big tables. Record DB size (`pg_database_size`).
3. NAS agents buffer to a local WAL/SQLite queue, so they need not be stopped —
   any rows they write to OLD after the snapshot are a small, regenerating
   telemetry gap; their unflushed buffer flushes to NEW after repoint (Phase 5).
   Note the snapshot time so the gap is understood.

## Phase 1 — Prepare the target project

Enable the extensions the schema depends on, in the same schemas as source
(verified installed on source: `pg_partman`@public, `pg_cron`@pg_catalog,
`pg_net`@public, `supabase_vault`@vault, `pgcrypto`/`uuid-ossp`/
`pg_stat_statements`@extensions, `plpgsql`). Most are created by the schema dump
or pre-exist on Supabase; create any missing ones via `apply_migration` (runs as
the privileged role). Confirm with `list_extensions(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)`.

## Phase 2 — Dump the source

Run from the scratch dir against `SOURCE_DB_URL`:

```bash
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f roles.sql  --role-only
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f schema.sql
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f data.sql   --use-copy --data-only
```

Then capture the out-of-band objects the above three files do **not** include:

- **Auth users** (needed for login + RLS + FK references):
  `pg_dump "$SOURCE_DB_URL" --data-only --schema=auth -t auth.users -t auth.identities -t auth.mfa_factors -f auth_data.sql`
  (enumerate actual non-empty `auth.*` tables first; keep it to user data, not
  Supabase-managed migration/bookkeeping tables).
- **pg_cron jobs** — dump the live job set so schedules/commands match exactly:
  `SELECT jobname, schedule, command FROM cron.job ORDER BY jobid;`
  Expect 8 jobs (renamed by migration 00034): `partition-maintenance`,
  `anomaly-detection`, `daily-health`, `process-ai-responses`,
  `sync-anomaly-detection`, `sync-health-analysis`, `weekly-sync-report`,
  `sync-remediation`. Generate `cron.schedule(...)` statements from the result.
- **Vault secret** — read the decrypted value from source to re-insert in target:
  `SELECT name, decrypted_secret FROM vault.decrypted_secrets WHERE name='smon_openai_api_key';`
  (If decryption is blocked, ask the owner for the OpenAI key value instead.)

For the very large data dump, prefer speed/robustness: dump in directory format
with parallelism for the big tables, or pipe `pg_dump | psql` per large table, to
avoid a single tens-of-GB SQL file. Validate disk before choosing; `--use-copy`
plain SQL is the safe default if disk allows.

## Phase 3 — Restore into the target

Canonical Supabase restore order, single transaction, triggers/FKs deferred:

```bash
psql --single-transaction --variable ON_ERROR_STOP=1 \
  --file roles.sql \
  --file schema.sql \
  --command 'SET session_replication_role = replica' \
  --file data.sql \
  --dbname "$TARGET_DB_URL"
```

Then load `auth_data.sql` (also with `session_replication_role = replica`).
`roles.sql` should be near-empty (no custom roles); ignore "already exists" on
Supabase default roles if any. After load, reset sequences to max(id) for any
serial PKs that were data-loaded.

## Phase 4 — Recreate out-of-band objects on target

1. **Vault secret**: `SELECT vault.create_secret('<value>', 'smon_openai_api_key');`
   Verify `smon_get_openai_key()` returns it.
2. **pg_cron jobs**: replay the 8 `cron.schedule(...)` statements captured in
   Phase 2. Verify `SELECT jobname, schedule FROM cron.job;` shows all 8.
3. **pg_partman**: confirm `part_config` rows restored (4 parents) and run one
   maintenance pass — `CALL public.run_maintenance_proc();` — then confirm it
   creates the next premake partitions without error.
4. Sanity-check `exec_sql` and the SECURITY-DEFINER `smon_*` functions exist and
   `service_role` has EXECUTE where expected.
5. Run `get_advisors(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)` for security/performance lints.

## Phase 5 — Repoint the application

New project values to wire in (fetch at execution):
- URL: `https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co`
- anon/publishable key: via `get_publishable_keys` / `supabase projects api-keys`
- service_role (secret) key: via `supabase projects api-keys --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`

**a. Repo + docs** (single ref swap `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` → `aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`):
`app/.env.example`, `apps/web/.env.example`, `deploy/synology/{nas-1,nas-2,.env.agent}.example`,
`scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs` (DEFAULT_SUPABASE_URL), `apply-migrations.ps1`,
`AGENTS.md`, `docs/{architecture,deployment,development}.md`,
`apps/relay/RECOVERY_PROMPT.md`. Commit on a branch.

**b. Web app image (baked at build!)**: `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and
`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are compiled into the bundle via
`.github/workflows/web-image.yml` build args from GitHub Secrets — a Coolify
runtime change does **not** repoint them. So: `gh secret set` both values →
trigger the workflow (`gh workflow run web-image.yml`) → wait for the new image.

**c. Coolify**: set runtime env on the web service — `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`
(and `SUPABASE_URL`/`NEXT_PUBLIC_*` if also set at runtime) to the new project —
then redeploy so it pulls the freshly built image.

**d. NAS agents (both)**: update each agent's `SUPABASE_URL` +
`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY` and restart the container. On restart they flush their
buffered queue into the new project. Repoint NAS-1 and NAS-2.

**e. Edge function (optional)**: only if push is wanted — `supabase functions
deploy send-push --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` and set its
`SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` secrets. Skipping changes nothing
today (it isn't deployed on the old project either).

## Phase 6 — Verify end-to-end

- **Data parity**: compare per-table counts target vs. the Phase 0 baseline
  (allow only the known post-snapshot telemetry delta). Spot-check a few config
  tables (`ai_settings`, `issues`, `copilot_*`, `facts`, `capability_state`) for
  exact equality.
- **App**: web app loads, login works (auth users migrated), dashboards render.
- **Agents**: confirm fresh rows landing in the new project's `metrics` /
  `nas_logs` for both NAS units (proves write path repointed).
- **In-DB automation**: confirm cron jobs are scheduled and a maintenance run +
  an `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` test call succeed (proves pg_cron + pg_net +
  vault wired). Run `scripts/check-dashboard-data.mjs` against the new URL/key.

## Phase 7 — Decommission / rollback

- Keep the old Ohio project **paused but intact** for a retention window as the
  rollback. Rollback = revert env on Coolify + NAS agents (and GH secrets +
  rebuild) to the old project; data written to NEW during the window would be the
  only loss.
- Once stable, owner can rename/pause/delete the old project. Separately, the
  pre-existing leaked-secrets rotation item still stands and is unaffected here.

## Risks / watch-items

- **Baked anon key**: forgetting the rebuild leaves the web bundle pointing at
  Ohio even after Coolify env changes — most likely footgun.
- **Cross-region volume**: tens of GB Ohio→sandbox→Virginia; size the dump
  strategy to disk/bandwidth, use `--use-copy`/parallel, expect a multi-hour
  data phase.
- **Vault + cron are silent if missed**: AI analyses/health just stop with no
  app error. Phase 4 + Phase 6 explicitly test them.
- **Auth**: if users aren't migrated, nobody can log in to the web app.
- Keep `SET session_replication_role = replica` for both data loads or FK/trigger
  ordering will fail the restore.

  SSH creds) — they hold their own `SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY`.
- Working scratch dir with tens of GB free (verify `df -h`).

## Phase 0 — Freeze (coordinated with owner)

1. Owner pauses the **web app** (stops web-originated writes).
2. Capture source baseline row counts for the verification diff:
   `SELECT relname, n_live_tup FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY 1;` plus exact
   counts for the big tables. Record DB size (`pg_database_size`).
3. NAS agents buffer to a local WAL/SQLite queue, so they need not be stopped —
   any rows they write to OLD after the snapshot are a small, regenerating
   telemetry gap; their unflushed buffer flushes to NEW after repoint (Phase 5).
   Note the snapshot time so the gap is understood.

## Phase 1 — Prepare the target project

Enable the extensions the schema depends on, in the same schemas as source
(verified installed on source: `pg_partman`@public, `pg_cron`@pg_catalog,
`pg_net`@public, `supabase_vault`@vault, `pgcrypto`/`uuid-ossp`/
`pg_stat_statements`@extensions, `plpgsql`). Most are created by the schema dump
or pre-exist on Supabase; create any missing ones via `apply_migration` (runs as
the privileged role). Confirm with `list_extensions(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)`.

## Phase 2 — Dump the source

Run from the scratch dir against `SOURCE_DB_URL`:

```bash
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f roles.sql  --role-only
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f schema.sql
supabase db dump --db-url "$SOURCE_DB_URL" -f data.sql   --use-copy --data-only
```

Then capture the out-of-band objects the above three files do **not** include:

- **Auth users** (needed for login + RLS + FK references):
  `pg_dump "$SOURCE_DB_URL" --data-only --schema=auth -t auth.users -t auth.identities -t auth.mfa_factors -f auth_data.sql`
  (enumerate actual non-empty `auth.*` tables first; keep it to user data, not
  Supabase-managed migration/bookkeeping tables).
- **pg_cron jobs** — dump the live job set so schedules/commands match exactly:
  `SELECT jobname, schedule, command FROM cron.job ORDER BY jobid;`
  Expect 8 jobs (renamed by migration 00034): `partition-maintenance`,
  `anomaly-detection`, `daily-health`, `process-ai-responses`,
  `sync-anomaly-detection`, `sync-health-analysis`, `weekly-sync-report`,
  `sync-remediation`. Generate `cron.schedule(...)` statements from the result.
- **Vault secret** — read the decrypted value from source to re-insert in target:
  `SELECT name, decrypted_secret FROM vault.decrypted_secrets WHERE name='smon_openai_api_key';`
  (If decryption is blocked, ask the owner for the OpenAI key value instead.)

For the very large data dump, prefer speed/robustness: dump in directory format
with parallelism for the big tables, or pipe `pg_dump | psql` per large table, to
avoid a single tens-of-GB SQL file. Validate disk before choosing; `--use-copy`
plain SQL is the safe default if disk allows.

## Phase 3 — Restore into the target

Canonical Supabase restore order, single transaction, triggers/FKs deferred:

```bash
psql --single-transaction --variable ON_ERROR_STOP=1 \
  --file roles.sql \
  --file schema.sql \
  --command 'SET session_replication_role = replica' \
  --file data.sql \
  --dbname "$TARGET_DB_URL"
```

Then load `auth_data.sql` (also with `session_replication_role = replica`).
`roles.sql` should be near-empty (no custom roles); ignore "already exists" on
Supabase default roles if any. After load, reset sequences to max(id) for any
serial PKs that were data-loaded.

## Phase 4 — Recreate out-of-band objects on target

1. **Vault secret**: `SELECT vault.create_secret('<value>', 'smon_openai_api_key');`
   Verify `smon_get_openai_key()` returns it.
2. **pg_cron jobs**: replay the 8 `cron.schedule(...)` statements captured in
   Phase 2. Verify `SELECT jobname, schedule FROM cron.job;` shows all 8.
3. **pg_partman**: confirm `part_config` rows restored (4 parents) and run one
   maintenance pass — `CALL public.run_maintenance_proc();` — then confirm it
   creates the next premake partitions without error.
4. Sanity-check `exec_sql` and the SECURITY-DEFINER `smon_*` functions exist and
   `service_role` has EXECUTE where expected.
5. Run `get_advisors(aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt)` for security/performance lints.

## Phase 5 — Repoint the application

New project values to wire in (fetch at execution):
- URL: `https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co`
- anon/publishable key: via `get_publishable_keys` / `supabase projects api-keys`
- service_role (secret) key: via `supabase projects api-keys --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`

**a. Repo + docs** (single ref swap `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` → `aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt`):
`app/.env.example`, `apps/web/.env.example`, `deploy/synology/{nas-1,nas-2,.env.agent}.example`,
`scripts/backfill-synobackup.mjs` (DEFAULT_SUPABASE_URL), `apply-migrations.ps1`,
`AGENTS.md`, `docs/{architecture,deployment,development}.md`,
`apps/relay/RECOVERY_PROMPT.md`. Commit on a branch.

**b. Web app image (baked at build!)**: `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` and
`NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` are compiled into the bundle via
`.github/workflows/web-image.yml` build args from GitHub Secrets — a Coolify
runtime change does **not** repoint them. So: `gh secret set` both values →
trigger the workflow (`gh workflow run web-image.yml`) → wait for the new image.

**c. Coolify**: set runtime env on the web service — `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`
(and `SUPABASE_URL`/`NEXT_PUBLIC_*` if also set at runtime) to the new project —
then redeploy so it pulls the freshly built image.

**d. NAS agents (both)**: update each agent's `SUPABASE_URL` +
`SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY` and restart the container. On restart they flush their
buffered queue into the new project. Repoint NAS-1 and NAS-2.

**e. Edge function (optional)**: only if push is wanted — `supabase functions
deploy send-push --project-ref aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` and set its
`SUPABASE_URL`/`SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` secrets. Skipping changes nothing
today (it isn't deployed on the old project either).

## Phase 6 — Verify end-to-end

- **Data parity**: compare per-table counts target vs. the Phase 0 baseline
  (allow only the known post-snapshot telemetry delta). Spot-check a few config
  tables (`ai_settings`, `issues`, `copilot_*`, `facts`, `capability_state`) for
  exact equality.
- **App**: web app loads, login works (auth users migrated), dashboards render.
- **Agents**: confirm fresh rows landing in the new project's `metrics` /
  `nas_logs` for both NAS units (proves write path repointed).
- **In-DB automation**: confirm cron jobs are scheduled and a maintenance run +
  an `smon_run_anomaly_detection()` test call succeed (proves pg_cron + pg_net +
  vault wired). Run `scripts/check-dashboard-data.mjs` against the new URL/key.

## Phase 7 — Decommission / rollback

- Keep the old Ohio project **paused but intact** for a retention window as the
  rollback. Rollback = revert env on Coolify + NAS agents (and GH secrets +
  rebuild) to the old project; data written to NEW during the window would be the
  only loss.
- Once stable, owner can rename/pause/delete the old project. Separately, the
  pre-existing leaked-secrets rotation item still stands and is unaffected here.

## Risks / watch-items

- **Baked anon key**: forgetting the rebuild leaves the web bundle pointing at
  Ohio even after Coolify env changes — most likely footgun.
- **Cross-region volume**: tens of GB Ohio→sandbox→Virginia; size the dump
  strategy to disk/bandwidth, use `--use-copy`/parallel, expect a multi-hour
  data phase.
- **Vault + cron are silent if missed**: AI analyses/health just stop with no
  app error. Phase 4 + Phase 6 explicitly test them.
- **Auth**: if users aren't migrated, nobody can log in to the web app.
- Keep `SET session_replication_role = replica` for both data loads or FK/trigger
  ordering will fail the restore.

toolUseResult.plan:
# Migrate Supabase backend: Ohio → Virginia

## Context

The Synology-monitor app's entire backend is a Supabase Postgres project in
**us-east-2 (Ohio)**: `qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn` ("SynoMon.old"). The owner wants it
moved to a freshly created, currently-empty project in **us-east-1 (Virginia)**:
`aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt` ("synomon", https://aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt.supabase.co).

Decisions confirmed by the owner:
- **Full-history copy** — everything, including the ~70M+ rows of telemetry.
- I perform the migration end-to-end. Owner provides: both DB passwords, the
  Coolify API token, and confirms the Supabase CLI is already authenticated
  (`SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN` is in the environment).
- The app can be **paused (~1h from now)** to get a clean, low-drift snapshot.

What "the backend" comprises (verified): ~100 tables (4 pg_partman-managed
time-series sets with weekly partitions p20260404…p20260606, plus low-volume
config/state tables), 8 pg_cron jobs, SECURITY-DEFINER functions incl.
`exec_sql`, a `supabase_vault` secret, RLS on every table, and a small number of
auth users. **No storage buckets in use. No edge functions deployed** (one
exists in code, `supabase/functions/send-push`, but was never deployed).

Outcome: an identical, fully-populated project in Virginia, with the live web
app and both NAS agents writing to it, the repo/docs updated, and the old
project left intact as a rollback.

## Why dump/restore (not migration replay)

The 41 migration files build the schema, but replaying them then `COPY`-ing data
risks partition-routing mismatches (pg_partman premake vs. the exact historical
weekly partitions that exist in source). A structural `pg_dump` captures the
exact final state — every child partition, function, index, RLS policy, and the
`part_config` rows — so it is the faithful path. Three things live *outside* the
dumped `public` schema and must be recreated explicitly: **pg_cron jobs** (`cron`
schema), the **vault secret** (encrypted per-project), and **auth users**.

## Prerequisites to collect at execution start

content.text:
Key facts: source DB is **29 GB**, **only 1 auth user** (trivial to migrate), and the **live cron set is 5 jobs** (not the 8 from the migration audit — three were later dropped). The authoritative list is: `smon-partition-maintenance`, `anomaly-detection`, `daily-health`, `process-ai-responses`, `sync-anomaly-detection`. 29 GB on 40 GB free confirms I should **stream `pg_dump | psql`** rather than land a file.

Let me capture the baseline, enumerate non-empty auth tables, and stand up extensions on the empty target.

input.query:
select n.nspname as schema, c.relname as tbl, c.reltuples::bigint as est_rows
from pg_class c join pg_namespace n on n.oid=c.relnamespace
where n.nspname='auth' and c.relkind='r'
and exists (select 1 from pg_class c2 where c2.oid=c.oid)
order by c.relname;

select string_agg(format('%s=%s', relname, n_live_tup), E'\n' order by relname) as counts
from pg_stat_user_tables where schemaname='public';
select name, default_version, installed_version from pg_available_extensions where installed_version is not null order by name;

content.subject: Phase 0: Freeze + capture source baseline
content.description: Coordinate app pause with owner. Capture per-table row counts, big-table exact counts, DB size, snapshot timestamp from source qnjimovrsaacneqkggsn for the verification diff.

content.subject: Phase 1: Prepare target extensions
content.description: Enable pg_partman, pg_cron, pg_net, supabase_vault, pgcrypto, uuid-ossp, pg_stat_statements on target aaxtrlfpnoutziwhshlt in matching schemas. Confirm via list_extensions.

content.subject: Phase 2: Dump source (roles/schema/data/auth/cron/vault)
content.description: supabase db dump roles+schema; stream/dump data; pg_dump auth users; capture cron.job statements and vault secret value from source.

content.subject: Phase 3: Restore into target
content.description: psql restore roles+schema+data with session_replication_role=replica; load auth data; reset sequences.

content.subject: Phase 4: Recreate out-of-band objects
content.description: Recreate vault secret, 8 pg_cron jobs, verify pg_partman maintenance, exec_sql/SECURITY DEFINER fns, run get_advisors.

content.subject: Phase 5: Repoint app (repo/GH/Coolify/NAS)
content.description: Swap repo refs on branch; gh secret set + rebuild image; Coolify runtime env + redeploy; repoint both NAS agents.

content.subject: Phase 6: Verify end-to-end
content.description: Data parity vs baseline; web login; fresh agent rows in new project; cron+pg_net+vault automation test; check-dashboard-data.mjs.

content.text: Phase 1 done. Let me verify the extensions landed and check the repo's git state (the earlier `git -C` error needs resolving before Phase 5 commits).

content.content:
=== .github/workflows/web-image.yml ===
name: Publish Web Image

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    paths:
      - ".github/workflows/web-image.yml"
      - "apps/web/**"
      - "packages/shared/**"
      - "package.json"
      - "pnpm-workspace.yaml"
      - "turbo.json"
  workflow_dispatch:

env:
  IMAGE_NAME: ghcr.io/${{ github.repository_owner }}/synology-monitor-web

jobs:
  build-and-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write

    steps:
      - name: Check out repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # PLAN.md §9.7 — hard build blockers. If these fail the image is never
      # built/pushed and Coolify is never triggered, so a cache-hostile prompt
      # ordering, collapsed multi-turn history, or an un-normalized provider
      # usage field cannot reach production.
      - name: Set up Node
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 22

      - name: Install pnpm
        run: npm install -g pnpm@9

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pnpm install --no-frozen-lockfile

      - name: AI cache CI guards
        run: pnpm --filter @synology-monitor/web run guard:ai

      - name: Set up QEMU
        uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Log in to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract image metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
          tags: |
            type=raw,value=latest
            type=sha
            type=ref,event=branch

      - name: Build and push web image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v6
        with:
          context: .
          file: ./apps/web/Dockerfile
          platforms: linux/amd64
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
          build-args: |
            NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=${{ secrets.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL }}
            NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=${{ secrets.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY }}
            BUILD_SHA=${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Trigger Coolify redeploy
        run: |
          curl -s -X GET \
            "http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/deploy?uuid=${{ secrets.COOLIFY_WEBHOOK_UUID }}&force=false" \
            -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.COOLIFY_TOKEN }}"

=== supabase client files (env usage) ===
--- apps/web/src/lib/supabase/client.ts ---
5:    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
6:    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!
--- apps/web/src/lib/supabase/server.ts ---
8:    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
9:    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!,
--- apps/web/src/lib/supabase/admin.ts ---
4:  const url = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL;
5:  const serviceRoleKey = process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY;
8:    throw new Error("NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY are required for admin workflows.");

=== Dockerfile ARG/ENV supabase ===
27:ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
28:ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
29:ARG BUILD_SHA
30:ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
31:ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
32:ENV BUILD_SHA=$BUILD_SHA
48:ENV NODE_ENV=production
49:ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
50:ENV PORT=3000
51:ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"

content.text:
I now have the entire cutover mechanism mapped:

- **Web image**: GH Actions `web-image.yml` (push-to-main or `workflow_dispatch`) bakes `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`/`ANON_KEY` from GH **secrets**, pushes to GHCR, then auto-triggers Coolify redeploy at `http://178.156.180.212:8000/api/v1/deploy`.
- **Runtime**: `admin.ts` reads `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` + `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL` from the Coolify `.env`.
- I can do GH-secret + rebuild + NAS repoint myself (NAS creds are in the Coolify `.env`).

Let me stage the runner script + cron SQL so execution is one command once I have the passwords.

input.command:
cd /tmp/supamig
# Cron jobs to recreate post-restore (exact live set from source cron.job)
cat > recreate_cron.sql <<'EOF'
select cron.schedule('smon-partition-maintenance','0 7 * * *','select public.run_maintenance_proc()');
select cron.schedule('anomaly-detection','*/15 * * * *','SELECT public.run_anomaly_detection()');
select cron.schedule('daily-health','0 12 * * *','SELECT public.run_daily_health()');
select cron.schedule('process-ai-responses','* * * * *','SELECT public.process_ai_responses()');
select cron.schedule('sync-anomaly-detection','*/15 * * * *','SELECT public.detect_sync_anomalies()');
EOF

# Main migration runner: expects SRC_DB_URL and TGT_DB_URL in env
cat > migrate.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
: "${SRC_DB_URL:?set SRC_DB_URL}"; : "${TGT_DB_URL:?set TGT_DB_URL}"
cd /tmp/supamig
log(){ echo "[$(date -u +%H:%M:%S)] $*"; }

log "connectivity check"
psql "$SRC_DB_URL" -tAc "select 'src ok '||current_database()" 
psql "$TGT_DB_URL" -tAc "select 'tgt ok '||current_database()"

log "dump roles (--role-only)"
supabase db dump --db-url "$SRC_DB_URL" -f roles.sql --role-only
log "dump schema (public/user schemas, managed excluded)"
supabase db dump --db-url "$SRC_DB_URL" -f schema.sql
log "schema lines: $(wc -l < schema.sql)"

log "restore roles + schema into target"
psql "$TGT_DB_URL" -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 --single-transaction -f roles.sql -f schema.sql

log "stream public DATA  src -> tgt (triggers/FK deferred)"
{ echo "set session_replication_role=replica;"; \
  pg_dump "$SRC_DB_URL" --data-only --schema=public --no-owner --no-privileges; } \
  | psql "$TGT_DB_URL" -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1

log "stream AUTH user data (users + identities)"
{ echo "set session_replication_role=replica;"; \
  pg_dump "$SRC_DB_URL" --data-only -t auth.users -t auth.identities --no-owner --no-privileges; } \
  | psql "$TGT_DB_URL" -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1

log "DONE dump/restore"
EOF
chmod +x migrate.sh
echo "staged:"; ls -la /tmp/supamig; echo "--- recreate_cron.sql ---"; cat recreate_cron.sql
input.description: Stage migration runner and cron SQL
